analogy: Think of "Women Change the World" through the chapter's own opening images: 48 Social and Political Life Women Change the World In the previous chapter, we saw how womens work in the home is not recognised as work. We also read how doing household work and taking care of family members is a full time job and... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 48 Social and Political Life, 5CHAPTER, 49 Chapter 5: Women Change the World, 50 Social and Political Life. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Women Change the World is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 48 Social and Political Life Women Change the World In the previous chapter, we saw how womens work in the home is not recognised as work.

The text explains that we also read how doing household work and taking care of family members is a full time job and there are no specific hours at which it begins or ends.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will look at work outside the home, and understand how some occupations are seen to be more suitable for men than for women.

The text explains that we will also learn about how women struggle for equality.

The text explains that getting an education was, and still is, one way in which new opportunities were created for women.

The text explains that this chapter will also briefly trace the different types of efforts made by the womens movement to challenge discrimination in more recent years.

The text explains that 49 A farmer A factory worker A nurse A scientist A pilot A teacher Are there more images of men than women?

The text explains that in what kinds of jobs were there more images of men than women?

The text explains that category Male image Female image Teacher Farmer Factory worker Nurse Scientist Pilot Who does what work?

The text explains that draw images of the following  See what images your class drew by filling in the table below.

The text explains that add up the number of male and female images separately for each occupation.

The text explains that 50 Social and Political Life 83.6 per cent of working women in India are engaged in agricultural work.

The text explains that their work includes planting, weeding, harvesting and threshing.

The text explains that yet, when we think of a farmer we only think of a man.

The text explains that source: NSS 61st Round (200405) Category Male image Female image Teacher 5 25 Farmer 30 0 Factory worker 25 5 Nurse 0 30 Scientist 25 5 Pilot 27 3 How does your class exercise compare with Rosie Maams class exercise?

The text explains that she did the same exercise in her class and here is the result.

The text explains that her philosophy is simple  As long as I am having fun without harming anyone, as long as I am doing well and helping my parents, why should I not lead a lifestyle of my choice? (Adapted from Driving Her Train by Neeta Lal, Womens Features Service) Fewer opportunities and rigid expectations A lot of the children in Rosie Maams class drew women as nurses and men as army officers.

The text explains that the reason they did this is because they feel that outside the home too, women are good at only certain jobs.

The text explains that 52 Social and Political Life Read the story below and answer the questions  If you were Xavier, what subject would you choose and why?

The text explains that in your experience, what are some of the other pressures that boys experience?

The text explains that xavier was happy with the results of his Class X board exams.Though his marks in Science and Maths were not high, he had done well in his favourite subjects  History and Languages.

The text explains that it is important to understand that we live in a society in which all children face pressures from the world around them.

The text explains that for example, in the pottery trade, women collected the mud and prepared the earth for the pots.

The text explains that learning to read and write led some women to question the situation of women in society.

The text explains that despite this, there continue to be many girls who leave school for reasons of poverty, inadequate schooling facilities and discrimination.

The text explains that according to the 1961 census, about 40 per cent of all boys and men Chapter 5: Women Change the World 55 Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) www.in.undp.org

The text explains that 56 Social and Political Life Level All SC ST Boys Girls Total Boys Girls Total Boys Girls Total Primary (Classes 1-5) 4.36 3.88 4.13 4.71 4.20 4.46 7.02 6.84 6.93 Upper Primary (Classes 6-8) 3.49 4.60 4.03 5.00 6.03 5.51 8.48 8.71 8.59 Secondary (Classes 9-10) 17.21 16.88 17.06 19.64 19.05 19.36 24.94 24.40 24.68 Source: Educational Statistics at a Glance, MHRD, 2018 What percentage of children leave school at the upper primary level?

The text explains that there are several reasons why children from Dalit, Adivasi and Muslim communities leave school.

The text explains that womens movement Women and girls now have the right to study and go to school.

The text explains that chapter 5: Women Change the World Find out about the Beti Bachao Beti Padhao campaign launched in 2014.

The text explains that eventually, this became a public issue in the newspapers and society, and the dowry laws were changed to punish families who seek dowry.

The text explains that raising Awareness An important part of the womens movements work is to raise public awareness on womens rights issues.

The text explains that how do you think stereotypes, about what women can or cannot do, affect womens right to equality?

The text explains that list one reason why learning the alphabet was so important to women like Rashsundari Devi, Ramabai and Rokeya.

The text explains that 3. Poor girls drop out of school because they are not interested in getting an education. Re-read the last paragraph on page 62 and explain why this statement is not true.

The text explains that for example, in this chapter, we saw how boys and girls are made to take certain subjects not because he or she has an aptitude for it, but because they are either boys or girls.

The text explains that the focus is on explaining the strong links between media and technology and media and big business.

The text explains that it explains how the media sets the agenda through influencing our perception of issues worth devoting time and attention to, a n d i s s u e s t h a t a r e n e g le c t e d o r overridden.

The text explains that chapter 6 foregrounds the widespread effects of the media, and attempts to connect the issues under discussion to the learners own lives.

The text explains that at the end of the chapter, we expect the learner to recognise the role of big business in the media coverage of events  the way news is selected for coverage, and the explicit/ implicit dimensions of that coverage.

The text explains that these ideas can be strengthened by selecting examples from actual advertisements and structuring similar questions around them.

The text explains that this chapter concludes by linking its contents to the idea of democracy.

The text explains that it emphasises, through using examples of local media as well as social advertising, how mainstream media and advertising tend to favour those who have greater financial as well as social resources.

The text explains that this point can be reinforced in the classroom by using local examples of media stories, as well as posing questions about the ways in which advertising is changing what is locally available as well as locally valued.

The text explains that we use two fictitious news reports to demonstrate that there is seldom just one version of a story or an event.

The text explains that building on this, we expect the learner to develop the skills required to critically analyse a newspaper report or a TV story through scrutinising the information provided, as well as understanding the logic behind the exclusion of certain perspectives.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II, Chapter 5: Women Change the World

concise_notes.definition: While a Muslim girl is likely to stay in school for around three years, girls from other communities spend around four years in school.

schemes.point: She went on to set up a Mission in Khedgaon near Pune in 1898, where widows and poor women were encouraged not only to become literate... (NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II Ch5 pg 5)

schemes.name: Protesting The
schemes.point: Protesting The womens movement raises its voice when violations against women take place or for example, when a law or policy acts against their interests. (NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II Ch5 pg 11)

schemes.point: Every year, on 14 August, several thousand people gather at Wagah on the border of India and Pakistan and hold a cultural programme. (NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II Ch5 pg 12)

schemes.point: It happens when people or organisations act on their prejudices. (NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II Ch5 pg 13)

prelims_traps: Do not treat 48 Social and Political Life and 5CHAPTER as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Women Change the World; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Women Change the World through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 48 Social and Political Life and 5CHAPTER to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
